summary: Reviewed the host briefing integration, shared house-rules extraction, display refactor, and first-game data flow against the spec. The main architectural risk is that a shared rules source now feeds the public display, but the refactor does not preserve the previous display contract exactly. Host state ownership and client/server boundaries otherwise mostly fit the existing App Router/Supabase patterns.
appears_sound: `PreGameBriefing` is stateless and receives `game`, `currentSnowballPot`, and `isFirstGameOfSession` as props, keeping data loading outside the presentation component in `src/components/host/pre-game-briefing.tsx:17`.

`GameControl` derives the briefing visibility from `currentGameState.numbers_called_count === 0`, so the briefing naturally reappears after undoing back to zero in `src/app/host/[sessionId]/[gameId]/game-control.tsx:786`.
The colour-name helper is pure shared logic with a native Node test covering valid and invalid inputs in `src/lib/colour-name.test.ts:17`.

findings.description: The shared `HOUSE_RULES` extraction changes the public display's rule text, violating the data-source-only contract for `/display`. Because the shared lib now feeds both host and TV display, this creates a public-surface regression despite the spec's no-display-change boundary.
findings.evidence: The removed display JSX rendered `they're` in `src/app/display/[sessionId]/display-ui.tsx:477`, but `HOUSE_RULES` now stores `they’re` in `src/lib/house-rules.ts:32` and display maps that shared value directly at `src/app/display/[sessionId]/display-ui.tsx:496`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: If the acceptance gate is visual only, the glyph difference may be judged minor, but it is not byte-identical text and can produce a pixel-level diff.

findings.description: The new first-game calculation assumes the loaded game belongs to the route session, but the page fetches `game` by `gameId` alone and computes first-game from `sessionId`. A stale or mismatched URL can render session context and house-rule gating for one session while controlling a game from another.
findings.evidence: The game query is scoped only by `.eq('id', gameId)` at `src/app/host/[sessionId]/[gameId]/page.tsx:40`, while the first-game query uses `.eq('session_id', sessionId)` at `src/app/host/[sessionId]/[gameId]/page.tsx:72` and compares indexes at `src/app/host/[sessionId]/[gameId]/page.tsx:77`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: Normal in-app navigation may always generate matching session/game IDs, so this may only be reachable through malformed URLs.
findings.what_would_confirm: Scope the game query with `.eq('session_id', sessionId)` or verify all route entry points and actions reject mismatched session/game IDs.

empty_categories: No client/server boundary leak found; the new host briefing imports only types, pure helpers, and presentation code into the client tree.
No schema, RLS, or persistence boundary changes were introduced by the implementation pack.
No shared mutable state or duplicate persisted state was introduced; briefing visibility is derived from existing `game_states` data.
No circular dependencies are visible among the changed files.
